stories.headline: Denver mayor orders police to detain ICE officers using excessive force
stories.summary:
Denver Mayor Mike Johnston on Thursday announced a new executive order outlining how the city will respond to federal immigration enforcement operations, including authorizing local police to detain ICE officers observed using excessive force against Denver residents. Johnston said Denver officers would investigate and prosecute any ICE agent who assaults or kills a civilian, regardless of federal government response.

Denver Police Chief Ron Thomas said all law enforcement officers have a statutory obligation to intervene when they witness illegal or excessive force by any officer, including federal agents, though he emphasized the first step would be de-escalation. City Attorney Miko Brown said enforcement options under the executive order could range from civil penalties to criminal charges to court injunctions, and would apply to all city-owned property.

Denver City Council President Amanda Sandoval said ICE is already operating in the city, citing reports of families being separated and workers taken from construction sites. Johnston framed the order as protective rather than provocative, stating that emergency responders would always provide lifesaving aid regardless of who caused an injury, and that no federal officer would be permitted to obstruct that effort.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis moves to permanently block ICE subpoena for immigrant sponsors' employment records
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis has asked a Denver court to issue a permanent injunction blocking him from releasing employment records that U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement subpoenaed roughly a year ago. The records cover employment details for sponsors of unaccompanied immigrant children who lack legal status, and Polis' attorneys argued that ICE's inaction and the passage of time suggest the agency no longer needs the information.

The move would end a lawsuit brought by former state Department of Labor and Employment worker Scott Moss, who sued Polis last June to block compliance with the subpoena. The suit, joined by the Colorado WINS union and the nonprofit law firm Towards Justice, has temporarily prevented release of the records. Moss' legal team said they had not been given sufficient time to consider the offer before it was presented to the court.

Moss' attorney Laura Wolf said she was glad Polis had conceded there was no basis to respond to the subpoena and noted that CDLE has since shifted its policy to effectively block similar subpoenas without a court order. The state labor department told lawmakers in January that it had not received any ICE subpoenas since last summer.

stories.headline: Measles case linked to Denver International Airport and Littleton church; free vaccinations offered
stories.summary:
A traveler with a confirmed measles case passed through Denver International Airport and attended a church service in Littleton while infectious over the weekend, Colorado public health officials announced Tuesday. The passenger arrived on Frontier flight 1279 from Miami at 9:15 a.m. Saturday, and anyone in gate area A83 between 9:15 a.m. and noon may have been exposed.

The traveler then attended a service and reception at St. Mary's Catholic Church, 6853 S. Prince St. in Littleton, between 9:30 a.m. and 2 p.m. Sunday, before departing DIA again late Sunday night on Frontier flight 1280. Anyone at DIA between 9:30 p.m. Sunday and 1:30 a.m. Monday faces potential exposure. State officials noted that because the passenger was fully vaccinated, transmission risk is lower but still possible.

The Colorado Department of Public Health and Environment offered free measles vaccinations to people potentially exposed at the church, with clinics held Wednesday at the Arapahoe County Public Health Department and at St. Mary's. Colorado recorded 36 measles cases in 2025, the most in a single year since at least 1996, with most cases among unvaccinated individuals.

stories.headline: Douglas County wildfire burns more than 1,000 acres near Franktown, forces evacuations
stories.summary:
A wildfire scorched more than 1,081 acres of dry grassland south of Franktown on Tuesday after igniting near Dahlberg and Lake Gulch roads around 12:39 p.m. Tinder-dry fuels, drought conditions, and winds up to 20 mph allowed the Dahlberg fire to spread rapidly, prompting Douglas County sheriff's deputies to immediately begin evacuating nearby homes. Of the 20 homes threatened, 17 evacuated.

Cherry Valley Elementary School was ordered to hold students in place before releasing them early for parent pickup, with the school fully evacuated by 4 p.m. Firefighters from across the metro Denver area responded, including an air tanker from Colorado Springs and a helicopter from Broomfield. Full containment was reached just before 5 p.m., and no building or property damage was reported.

High winds also disrupted operations at Denver International Airport on Tuesday, with the FAA ordering a ground stop. Airlines delayed 385 flights and canceled 25 as of 6 p.m. Red flag warnings for critical fire weather conditions remained in effect for most of the Front Range and Eastern Plains on Wednesday, with sustained winds up to 35 mph and gusts up to 50 mph forecast.

stories.headline: Grass fire in Thornton injures five, prompts large-scale response and freeway closure
stories.summary:
A fast-moving grass fire ignited at 84th Avenue and Huron Street in Thornton on February 25 amid Red Flag conditions, burning approximately 10 acres along a green belt between a residential neighborhood and a commercial zone before being contained at 2:07 p.m. Fire Chief Stephen Kelley described the blaze as a dynamic, rapidly spreading event that challenged multiple departments across the north Denver metro area.

The fire prompted evacuations via door-to-door outreach from law enforcement and the full evacuation of a nearby high school as a precaution. I-25 was closed in the area due to dangerous smoke reducing visibility. Resources from Thornton, Westminster, Adams County, North Denver Metro, Northglenn, Federal Heights, South Adams, and other agencies responded to the scene.

Five people were injured, including four firefighters and one civilian, all with non-critical injuries. No residential structures were lost, though some businesses on the southern edge of the fire sustained damage. Crews remained overnight to monitor for re-ignition, and the cause of the fire is under investigation.

stories.headline: Two Tren de Aragua members sentenced to 20 years in federal prison for Denver jewelry store robbery
stories.summary:
Two members of the Venezuelan prison gang Tren de Aragua were sentenced to 20 years each in federal prison for their roles in the armed robbery of Joyeria El Ruby jewelry store in Denver's West Highland neighborhood in June 2024. Jean Torres-Ramon, 22, and Newman Castillo Delgado, 23, pleaded guilty to robbery and brandishing a firearm in furtherance of a crime of violence, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of Colorado announced.

The group is accused of entering the store at 5108 W. 38th Ave., aiming guns at employees, beating workers with weapons, and stealing nearly $4 million in gold and jewelry. A separate federal indictment alleges the robbery was approved by Tren de Aragua leadership to enrich the gang. Torres-Ramon was sentenced to 235 months and Castillo Delgado to 240 months, and both were ordered to pay $3.9 million in restitution.

Five additional suspects — Oswaldo Lozada-Solis, Jesus Daniel Lara Del Toro, Derek Alexander Dun-Vargas, Briley Ballera-Farias, and Edwuimar Nazareth Colina-Romero — still have open federal cases. The sentencings represent the first concluded prosecutions in the high-profile case that drew national attention to the gang's activities in the Denver area.

stories.headline: Denver man, 20, charged with first-degree murder in Valentine's Day house party shooting
stories.summary:
Denver Police arrested Yeanbraiker Yriarte-Valera, 20, in connection with a fatal shooting that occurred around 5 a.m. on February 14 at a residence in the 1500 block of West Maple Avenue. Yriarte-Valera faces a first-degree murder charge after police say he shot and killed an adult man found on the building's front porch suffering from multiple gunshot wounds. A woman was also hospitalized with a gunshot wound to the ankle.

According to a probable cause affidavit, officers responding to the scene found evidence of a large house party, including alcohol containers, large speakers, and a light display in the basement. Police recovered 25 spent 9mm casings in the street and 10 more on the first floor and staircase, along with bullet damage to neighboring structures. Witnesses reported multiple street fights in the area approximately 30 minutes before the shooting.

Spanish-speaking witnesses, interviewed with the help of a translating officer, corroborated that a fight involving multiple men and one woman preceded the gunfire. The incident involved a complex domestic situation, with a woman at the party encountering both a current and former boyfriend, leading to confrontations that culminated in the fatal shooting. The investigation is ongoing.

stories.headline: Denver Pavilions to undergo visioning process with national real estate panel; $100,000 contract awarded
stories.summary:
The Denver Downtown Development Authority will host an advisory services panel from the Urban Land Institute in Denver from April 13-17 to help determine the future of the Denver Pavilions mall and adjacent parking lots on 16th Street. The DDDA is paying ULI $100,000 for the panel's recommendations on how to proceed with the property it purchased late last year for $60 million combined.

The DDDA bought the 350,000-square-foot mall at 500 16th St. for $37 million and two nearby parking lots for $23 million in December after owner Gart Properties defaulted on the property's loan. The mall is currently about 60 percent leased, generating $5.3 million in annual rent, with the parking operations bringing in an additional $3.2 million per year. Gart Properties continues to manage the property under a $379,000 contract.

Ahead of the ULI visit, the DDDA has issued two requests for proposals — one seeking a repositioning vision from retail and entertainment leasing specialists, and another from architecture and urban design firms to reimagine the Pavilions as a centerpiece for a revitalized upper downtown. Chief Projects Officer Bill Mosher said the city's goal is to transfer the asset to the private sector as quickly as possible, preserving flexibility for a new owner's vision.

stories.headline: Denver AI firm Rank One Computing goes public on Nasdaq, raising $24 million
stories.summary:
Denver-based artificial intelligence company Rank One Computing, known as ROC, made its public market debut Friday on the Nasdaq Capital Market at $6 per share under the ticker ROC, raising $24 million. Strong demand triggered an overallotment option allowing the underwriter to purchase an additional 600,000 shares. Shares closed Tuesday at $5.80, valuing the company at approximately $95 million.

ROC, founded in 2015 and headquartered at 1290 Broadway in Denver, specializes in image recognition technology including facial recognition, fingerprints, irises, tattoos, license plate reading, and weapon identification. The company has 42 employees and counts U.S. federal agencies, state and local law enforcement, global finance firms, and retail security companies among its dozens of paying customers. The company claims its Vision AI platform can read fingerprints 500 times faster than competing vendors in testing.

The IPO comes one week after Denver lost major AI company Palantir Technologies, which abruptly relocated its headquarters to Miami. ROC plans to use the proceeds to hire engineering, sales, and support staff and to accelerate training and deployment of its Vision AI algorithms. The company's public offering is the second IPO by a Colorado company in 2026, following Greenwood Village-based satellite maker York Space Systems, which raised $629 million in January.

stories.headline: DIA broadens Peña Boulevard plan to include mass transit alternatives amid environmental review
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport officials have expanded their approach to addressing traffic congestion on Peña Boulevard to include public transit options alongside the previously planned roadway widening. The shift occurred as a required National Environmental Policy Act review began last month for the project to overhaul the mostly two-lane freeway connecting Denver with the airport. Funding for the project, expected to cost hundreds of millions of dollars, remains uncertain.

Expanding RTD A Line train frequency by adding track and constructing bus-only lanes — alternatives previously excluded from DIA's Peña Master Plan — will now be evaluated as core options. The average drive time along the 11-mile corridor from Interstate 70 to DIA's main terminal has tripled from eight minutes to 24 minutes, and peak-period backups with rear-end accidents can delay drivers by more than 30 minutes. Environmental groups including GreenLatinos and Greater Denver Transit had challenged the road-widening-only approach, arguing highway expansions induce more traffic over time.

RTD currently runs A Line trains to DIA every 15 minutes at peak hours and about every 30 minutes in evenings and early mornings. The Federal Aviation Administration must ultimately approve DIA's final decision following the NEPA review process.

stories.headline: Nuggets defeat Celtics 103-84 in season-best defensive performance
stories.summary:
The Denver Nuggets ended a stretch of six losses in nine games with a 103-84 victory over the Boston Celtics at Ball Arena on Wednesday, holding an opponent under 100 points for only the third time this season. Nikola Jokic led Denver with 30 points, 12 rebounds, and six assists, while Tim Hardaway Jr. added 14 off the bench. The Nuggets outscored Boston 42-28 in the paint, 23-9 on second-chance points, and 14-6 on fast breaks.

Denver limited the Celtics to 12-for-43 shooting from three-point range and held Jaylen Brown, who has carried an MVP-level season in Jayson Tatum's absence, to 23 points on 21 field goal attempts. Bruce Brown was credited with particularly strong perimeter defense on Brown, picking his pocket multiple times. Guard Julian Strawther, starting for the sixth consecutive game, contributed 12 points on 50-plus percent shooting.

Coach David Adelman said the win highlighted what the team must develop to contend deeper into the season. The Nuggets improved to 37-22 on the year. Jamal Murray, who had been listed as probable with right hamstring tightness, was available and played in the game.

stories.headline: Avalanche's Brock Nelson returns from Winter Olympics gold medal win; Nathan MacKinnon sits out with maintenance
stories.summary:
Colorado Avalanche forward Brock Nelson returned to the ice Wednesday night against the Utah Mammoth in Salt Lake City after helping the United States win the men's hockey gold medal at the 2026 Winter Olympics in Milan — the country's first gold in the event since the 1980 Miracle on Ice. Nelson, still wearing a bubble shield from a high-stick injury sustained during Olympic play, described the experience as something he will remember forever and noted his connection to U.S. hockey history: his uncle Dave Christian was on the 1980 team, and his grandfather and great-uncle played on the 1960 gold-medal squad.

Nelson addressed the controversy surrounding the team's visit to Washington, D.C. this week, during which 20 of the 25 players toured the White House and attended the State of the Union. Nelson skipped the trip, citing family priorities. He declined to take sides on the criticism directed at the team, saying both the men's and women's gold-medal teams should be proud of their accomplishments and that it was each player's right to choose whether to attend.

Nathan MacKinnon, who won silver with Canada after the U.S. beat Canada 2-1 in overtime in the gold-medal game, did not play Wednesday due to what the team called maintenance. It was the first game MacKinnon has missed this season. He leads the NHL with 40 goals and sits second in scoring with 93 points.

comic.teaser: "“Well, according to the dictionary, I’m just a large, flightless bird from Ea..." + 3 more
